title: Precision measurements at the interface between unitary and non-unitary encoding
abstract: We investigate precision scaling at the interface between unitary and non-unitary encoding under generalized noise including single-particle and collective dephasing and decay. Using linear response theory and the error propagation formula, we derive analytic precision expressions for both the unitary parameter $Ω$ and the dissipation strength $γ$. For unitary encoding, when the observable commutes with a Hermitian noise operator, the optimal encoding time is independent of $N$, yielding the Heisenberg limit $ΔΩ\propto 1 / N$; otherwise the precision degrades to the standard quantum limit or ceases to improve with $N$. For non-unitary encoding, when $[\hat{A}, \hat{O}] = 0$, the precision is insensitive to intrinsic dynamics and encoding time, scaling as $Δγ\propto \sqrt{γ/ \expval*{\hat{L}^\dagger \hat{L}}}$. Notably, for collective decay, the Dicke state reaches the Heisenberg limit $Δγ\propto 1 / N$, demonstrating that entanglement can enhance non-unitary estimation. Our results provide a unified framework and practical guidance for designing quantum metrology protocols in noisy environments.
summary: This paper develops a unified theory for quantum metrology under generalized noise, analyzing parameter estimation for both unitary and non-unitary dynamics. It derives analytic scaling laws showing that Heisenberg limited precision ($\propto 1/N$) is achievable in specific noisy regimes, even when dissipation is present. The findings offer practical guidance for designing robust quantum sensing protocols.
main_problem: The paper investigates how the precision of quantum parameter estimation scales when the encoding process transitions between unitary (coherent) and non-unitary (dissipative/noisy) dynamics.
main_result: Heisenberg scaling ($\propto 1/N$) can be achieved for both unitary and non-unitary estimations if specific commutation relations hold, demonstrating entanglement's role in enhancing metrology even under noise.
method: The authors employ linear response theory and the error propagation formula to derive analytic precision expressions, supplemented by numerical validation using the Discrete Truncated Wigner Approximation (DTWA).
model_or_system: The model involves quantum systems subjected to generalized noise, including single-particle and collective dephasing/decay. Dynamics are governed by non-Hermitian Hamiltonians incorporating both intrinsic evolution ($\Omega$) and dissipation ($\gamma$).
key_observables: Precision scaling for the unitary parameter $\Delta\Omega$ and the dissipation strength $\Delta\gamma$.
important_parameters: System size $N$, noise rates ($\gamma$), encoding time ($t$), and commutation relations between observables and noise operators.
main_assumptions: The derivations rely on linear response theory, cumulant expansions, and approximations up to first order in the noise parameters.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate precision dynamics for both unitary and non-unitary regimes, confirming Heisenberg scaling ($\propto 1/N$) under optimal conditions but showing degradation towards Standard Quantum Limit (SQL) or constant values otherwise.
paper_structure: The paper develops a unified framework by deriving analytic expressions for $\Delta\Omega$ and $\Delta\gamma$. It analyzes specific noise types (single-particle vs. collective) and compares the scaling laws based on whether observables commute with noise operators, providing practical guidance for protocol design.
why_interesting: This work provides crucial theoretical benchmarks for designing quantum sensors in realistic noisy environments by quantifying when entanglement can overcome decoherence to reach fundamental precision limits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Precision dynamics for various N under single- particle dephasing (a) and collective dephasing (c). The ini- tial state is the optimal two-axis twisted state with ⟨ˆJx⟩∝N and ⟨∆ˆJz⟩∝1. (a, c) The optimal encoding time t, indicated by pentagrams at the maximum curvature, is independent of N. (b, d) The precision scaling at the optimal time exhibits the Heisenberg scaling ∆Ω∝1/N. The dissipation rate is γ/γ0 = 0.1. The energy and time units are γ0 and γ−1 0 , re- spectively.

FIG. 2. Precision scaling at the optimal encoding time for var- ious N under single-particle dephasing (a) and collective de- phasing (b). The initial state is the optimal two-axis twisted state with ⟨ˆJy⟩∝N and ⟨∆ˆJx⟩∝1. The precision scaling degrades to ∆Ω∝1/ √

FIG. 3. Precision scaling at the optimal encoding time for var- ious N under single-particle decay (a) and collective decay (b). The initial state is the optimal two-axis twisted state with ⟨ˆJx⟩∝N and ⟨∆ˆJz⟩∝1. The precision scaling degrades to ∆Ω∝1/ √

FIG. 5. Precision scaling at the optimal encoding time for different total particle number N under non-unitary encoding ˆL = ˆJ−with intrinsic dynamics ˆA = ˆJz (a) or ˆA = ˆJ2 z (b), where the observable is ˆO = ˆJz. The initial state is the Dicke state |N, 0⟩z with eigenvalue 0 of ˆJz. The precision scales as the Heisenberg limit ∆γ ∝1/N. Ω/Ω0 = 0.1 for the intrinsic dynamics. The energy and the time units are Ω0 and Ω−1 0 , respectively.

FIG. 4. Precision dynamics for various N under non-unitary encoding with ˆL = ˆσj z, ˆO = ˆJx, and intrinsic dynamics ˆA = ˆJx (a) and ˆA = ˆJ2 x (c). The initial state is the eigenstate of ˆJx with eigenvalue N. The precision scaling at the optimal time, indicated by pentagrams at the maximum curvature, yields ∆γ ∝1/ √
